title: The Nervous System and Knitting: Why Repetitive Craft Calms the Brain
excerpt: Discover how repetitive crafts like knitting soothe your nervous system and promote trauma recovery through somatic regulation.
body:
Introduction: The Power of Repetitive Craft

Have you ever noticed how knitting or other repetitive crafts can bring a surprising sense of calm? For many who have tried therapy but still feel stuck in their trauma or anxiety, understanding the nervous system’s role in this calming effect can unlock new pathways to healing. This article explores the science behind why repetitive crafts, especially knitting, soothe the nervous system and support somatic trauma recovery.

The Nervous System 101: Understanding Your Body’s Alarm and Reset

The nervous system, particularly the autonomic nervous system (ANS), governs our stress responses and ability to regulate emotions. It includes two main branches: the sympathetic nervous system (SNS), which activates the fight, flight, or freeze response, and the parasympathetic nervous system (PNS), which promotes rest, digestion, and healing.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory deepens this understanding by introducing the social engagement system, a parasympathetic pathway that helps us feel safe and connected. When this system is activated, we experience calm and openness, essential for healing trauma (Porges, 2011).

Why Regulation Matters

Trauma, chronic stress, and anxiety can leave the nervous system stuck in a state of hyper- or hypo-arousal, making it hard to feel safe or calm. Bessel van der Kolk (2015) emphasized that trauma lives in the body, not just the mind, which means somatic approaches to regulation are key to recovery.

Knitting and the Nervous System: What’s the Connection?

Knitting is a rhythmic, repetitive activity that engages both the body and mind. These characteristics make it a powerful tool for nervous system regulation for several reasons:

  • Rhythmic Movement: The repetitive hand motions stimulate the vagus nerve, which plays a central role in activating the calming parasympathetic nervous system.
  • Focused Attention: Concentrating on the stitches can act as a form of mindfulness, helping interrupt the cycle of anxious thoughts.
  • Sense of Control: Creating something tangible provides a grounding experience, reinforcing safety and mastery.

Research Supporting Repetitive Craft and Regulation

A 2017 study in the British Journal of Occupational Therapy found that knitting reduced stress and anxiety in participants by decreasing heart rate and blood pressure, both markers of parasympathetic activation (Conrad & Adams, 2017). This physiological shift is exactly what trauma-informed somatic practitioners aim for.

"Knitting’s repetitive rhythm provides a somatic anchor that helps the nervous system shift out of survival mode and into restoration." — The Oracle Lover

Somatic Healing and the Role of Repetitive Crafts

Peter Levine, a pioneer in somatic trauma therapy, teaches that trauma recovery depends on the body’s ability to complete self-protective responses that were interrupted during the traumatic event (Levine, 2010). Repetitive crafts like knitting can facilitate this by:

  • Encouraging gentle, predictable movement that signals safety.
  • Helping regulate breathing and heart rate through rhythmic activity.
  • Providing a sensory-rich experience that reconnects the brain to the body.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy similarly emphasizes body awareness as crucial to trauma recovery. Knitting’s tactile and proprioceptive feedback supports this reconnection (Ogden, 2015).

How to Use Knitting as a Nervous System Regulation Tool

If you’re new to knitting or crafts, here’s how to integrate it into your self-directed healing work:

  • Start Simple: Choose basic stitches that are easy to repeat and don’t require intense focus.
  • Set a Calm Environment: Find a quiet, comfortable spot to reduce external stressors and distractions.
  • Focus on Breath and Movement: Notice how your hands move and how your breath changes with your rhythm.
  • Use it as a Mindful Break: When feeling overwhelmed, pause and engage in knitting to ground yourself.
  • Be Patient: Like any trauma recovery tool, it takes consistent practice to feel the full benefits.

Beyond Knitting: Other Repetitive Crafts That Calm

While knitting is a standout due to its tactile and rhythmic nature, other crafts can also support nervous system regulation:

  • Crocheting: Similar repetitive hand motions and focus.
  • Weaving: Engages both hands and provides visual feedback.
  • Beading: Small repetitive movements that require focus.
  • Painting or Drawing Patterns: Repetitive brush strokes or line drawing can induce a meditative state.

Each of these crafts shares the common thread of rhythm and repetition, essential for soothing the nervous system.

Integrating Science and Somatic Wisdom

Gabor Maté (2018) highlights that healing trauma requires creating safety and connection within the body. Repetitive crafts like knitting are not just hobbies; they are tools that create a somatic container for this safety to arise. Dan Siegel’s concept of mindsight—the ability to sense and regulate internal experiences—can be cultivated through mindful knitting, strengthening self-awareness and emotional regulation (Siegel, 2010).

Moreover, Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) therapy benefits from bilateral stimulation, a concept echoed in the alternating hand movements of knitting that can similarly aid processing trauma (Shapiro, 2017).

Conclusion: Crafting Calmness, Stitch by Stitch

Knitting is more than a creative outlet; it’s a scientifically grounded method for calming the nervous system and supporting somatic trauma recovery. By engaging in repetitive, rhythmic craft, you can gently shift your nervous system out of survival mode into a state of restoration and healing. If traditional talk therapy left you feeling stuck, integrating knitting or similar crafts into your self-directed healing may open new doors to calm and connection.

Remember, the journey to nervous system regulation is personal and nonlinear. But with tools like knitting, you have a powerful, accessible way to nurture your body’s innate capacity to heal.

References

  • Conrad, D., & Adams, C. (2017). The effects of knitting on anxiety and stress. British Journal of Occupational Therapy, 80(7), 441-447.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2015). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2010). Mindsight: The New Science of Personal Transformation. Bantam Books.
  • van der Kolk, B. A. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.

faqs.q: How does knitting affect the nervous system?
faqs.a: Knitting engages rhythmic, repetitive movements that stimulate the parasympathetic nervous system via the vagus nerve, promoting calm and reducing stress.

faqs.q: Can knitting help with trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Yes. Knitting provides somatic regulation by grounding you in your body and activating calming nervous system responses, which supports trauma healing as explained by somatic therapists like Peter Levine and Pat Ogden.

faqs.q: Is knitting a form of mindfulness?
faqs.a: Absolutely. The focused attention on repetitive stitches encourages mindfulness by keeping you present and interrupting anxious thought patterns.

faqs.q: What if I don’t know how to knit? Are there other options?
faqs.a: Definitely. Other repetitive crafts like crocheting, weaving, or beading offer similar calming effects through rhythmic, focused movement.
